metadata.title: They Were Paying the CIA
metadata.global_visual_style: Historical archival photo style, Kodachrome 60s–80s film grain, cinematic noir lighting, high-contrast shadows, desaturated Cold War palette with occasional bursts of amber and blue. 9:16 vertical framing. Fast cuts — visual change every 3 seconds minimum.
metadata.bg_music_note: No background music. Intentional — maximizes Shorts revenue pool allocation. All audio is narration and SFX only.

timeline.narration: For 70 years, 120 governments paid a Swiss company to keep their secrets safe.
timeline.visual_description: Black screen. Large white sans-serif text appears center frame: 'THEY WERE PAYING THE CIA.' Text snaps in on the first word, cold and stark. No other visual elements. Vertical 9:16 frame.

timeline.narration: The company was called Crypto AG.
timeline.visual_description: Vintage documentary-style world map, Europe centered, Switzerland highlighted with a soft golden pulse radiating outward. Kodachrome warm tones on aged parchment-texture map. Country borders in thin ink lines.

CIA seal — sharp eagle emblem — fills the frame, slightly over-exposed, institutional. Then cuts to an aerial photograph of Langley headquarters in muted 1970s Kodachrome tones. Flat, cold morning light.

timeline.narration: Iran. Argentina. The Vatican. Pakistan. India.
timeline.visual_description: Rapid staccato montage — each national flag fills the frame for half a second in sequence: Iran, Argentina, Vatican City, Pakistan, India. Flat dark background, flags slightly desaturated, Kodachrome grain over each cut.
timeline.sfx_prompt: each flag cut lands with a quiet sharp percussive tick

timeline.narration: Every message they sent through Crypto AG's machines — the CIA could read it.
timeline.visual_description: Extreme close-up of a mechanical cipher machine — brass rotors, key switches, type bars. Hands visible at the edges, pressing keys in deliberate rhythm. Warm amber light catches the metal. Kodachrome film grain, shallow depth of field.
timeline.sfx_prompt: rhythmic mechanical key presses, metal cipher rotor clicks, faint whirring

timeline.narration: The encryption looked real. It wasn't. The backdoor was built in from the start.
timeline.visual_description: Split screen — left: a foreign diplomat in formal dress transmitting a cable, expression confident; right: a CIA analyst at a Langley desk reading the decoded printout, expressionless. Cinematic noir lighting, 1970s institutional interiors. Kodachrome muted greens.
timeline.sfx_prompt: faint teletype machine sound, quiet office ambient

timeline.narration: During the Falklands War, the CIA read every Argentine naval communication in real time.
timeline.visual_description: Archival-style photograph of Argentine naval vessels at sea — grey water, overcast sky, military silhouettes. Horizon line low in frame. Desaturated Kodachrome, slight vignette, documentary news-reel feel.
timeline.sfx_prompt: distant ocean wind, faint ship engine hum

timeline.narration: Britain knew every Argentine move before it happened.
timeline.visual_description: British military command room — uniformed officers gathered around a large tactical map table, markers and flags positioned. WWII-era documentary photo style, muted olive greens and dark browns, Kodachrome warm grain.
timeline.sfx_prompt: muffled radio chatter, quiet pencil marks on paper

timeline.narration: The operation ran for 70 years. It was never detected.
timeline.visual_description: Overhead shot of towering stacks of manila folders and classified documents on a dark wooden surface. Top folder bears a heavy red CLASSIFIED stamp pressed deeply into it. Single hard overhead light source, everything else in shadow.
timeline.sfx_prompt: paper shifting, heavy rubber stamp impact

timeline.narration: It was only exposed in 2020, when the Washington Post published the CIA's own internal history.
timeline.visual_description: Washington Post front page, February 2020, laid flat on a dark wooden surface. Headline partially visible. Overhead camera, single hard light source creates strong shadows on the newsprint. Kodachrome warm paper tones.

timeline.narration: Quote: 'The intelligence coup of the century.'
timeline.visual_description: Close-up of a declassified document page. The phrase 'the intelligence coup of the century' is highlighted in bright yellow marker. Slow push-in zoom toward the highlighted text. Aged paper texture, Kodachrome cream tones.

timeline.narration: The man who built the machines? He made a gentleman's agreement with the CIA in 1951.
timeline.visual_description: Black and white photograph of Boris Hagelin — an older gentleman in a business suit, composed expression, 1950s formal portrait style. Frame slightly overexposed at edges, period-accurate vignette. Kodachrome warm overlay converts it to soft sepia.

timeline.narration: Crypto AG operated out of Switzerland — a country legally neutral in every conflict it was helping surveil.
timeline.visual_description: Exterior of a pristine, modern Swiss corporate building — clean glass facade, Swiss mountains visible in the background. Cold blue early morning light. Kodachrome landscape style. The building looks unremarkable, which is the point.

timeline.narration: 120 governments. 70 years. Paying to be watched.
timeline.visual_description: Slowly rotating globe on a dark background — Cold War-era cartographic style with muted blue oceans and cream landmasses. Red dots appear one by one across 120 countries, each pulsing softly. The globe continues to rotate as dots accumulate.

timeline.narration: The operation ended only because Crypto AG was quietly liquidated. Some of those governments still don't know what happened.
timeline.visual_description: Heavy steel bank vault door swings slowly shut. Extreme close-up of the locking mechanism — tumblers turning, bolts sliding into place. Deep blacks, single amber light catches the polished steel. Cinematic noir lighting.
timeline.sfx_prompt: heavy vault door boom, mechanical lock tumblers clicking into place, final silence

timeline.narration: The full 26-minute documentary is on the channel. Every government. Every cipher. Every year.
timeline.visual_description: Channel-branded card — the long-form Crypto AG documentary thumbnail displayed prominently with bold text overlay: 'FULL STORY: 26 MINUTES'. Chronicles of Eras channel name and logo visible. Direct, clean composition against dark background.

LOOP SETUP — identical to opening frame: Black screen. Large white sans-serif text: 'THEY WERE PAYING THE CIA.' Holds for 4 seconds before seamless replay. Mirrors the opening precisely for maximum loop rate.
